content:
The term **artificial intelligence** was coined in **1956** at the Dartmouth workshop, where researchers proposed that every aspect of learning and intelligence could be precisely described and simulated by machines.

Early decades emphasized **symbolic AI**: explicit rules, logic, knowledge representation, and search (GPS, expert systems like MYCIN, chess programs). Progress alternated with **AI winters** when promised generality outpaced hardware and data.

**Machine learning** rose in the 1990s–2000s as cheap compute and digitized data made statistical pattern learning competitive with hand-crafted rules for perception and ranking tasks.

**Deep learning** (2010s) and **generative models** (2020s) shifted public perception so that 'AI' often means large neural nets—yet the field's broader agenda (reasoning, planning, robotics) continues alongside.

This topic records that history at framing depth; algorithmic and product specifics belong in sibling and stage topics.

timeline.label: 1956 — Dartmouth workshop
timeline.description: Name 'artificial intelligence' adopted; symbolic approaches and search dominate early research.

timeline.label: 1980s — Expert systems
timeline.description: Rule-based AI commercialized in medicine and configuration; brittle knowledge engineering limits scale.

timeline.label: 1990s–2000s — Statistical ML
timeline.description: Learning from data rivals hand rules for speech, vision, and ranking; ML becomes the default engineering path.

timeline.label: 2010s–present — Deep & generative wave
timeline.description: Neural networks at scale and generative models reshape products; colloquial 'AI' narrows toward ML-based systems.
